list item a node represents — its stable identity across renders. React uses keys to match Fibers from the previous render against nodes in the new render output. A matching key means React reuses the existing Fiber and DOM node; a missing or changed key triggers a create-or-destroy cycle.

attrs.alt: Comparison of positional vs key-based reconciliation

content.text: Without keys, React matches children by

: the node at index 0 in the previous output is compared against the node at index 0 in the new output, regardless of what they represent. This is cheap and usually correct for static or append-only lists, but breaks correctness for lists that can be inserted into the middle, reordered, or prepended.

from the current children before walking the new children list. For each new child it looks up the key in this map. A hit means reuse (UPDATE path); a miss means creation (INSERT path). Any Fibers left in the map after the walk are deletions.
Keys are scoped to a parent — they only need to be unique among siblings, not globally. Two unrelated lists can each have a child with
State Lives in the Fiber, Not the DOM

) lives in the Fiber, not in the DOM. When React reuses a Fiber for a keyed item it also reuses that item's state. When it destroys a Fiber it discards the state. This is why positional matching produces stale state on reordered lists — the state that belonged to item A is now attached to the DOM node at item A's old position, which React handed to item B.
Good key sources are values that are unique per item and stable across renders:
Slugs or other stable business identifiers
Composite keys for pairs (e.g. userId + postId)
Avoid keys derived from render-time values:
— different key every render → force unmount+remount on every render
Array index when the list can be reordered or have items inserted anywhere but the end
The item value itself when values can repeat (two users named Alice)
Because changing a key destroys the old Fiber and creates a new one, you can use a key change as an
— a signal to React to throw away the old instance and start fresh. This is simpler than writing effect-based reset logic:
This pattern works for any component state reset — form fields clearing when switching accounts, tabs starting from their initial tab, wizards restarting from step one.
When a list is sorted, filtered, or reordered, keys tell React which items moved and which are new or deleted:
existing DOM nodes in the general case. It marks Fibers as needing to be placed (INSERTION effect) at their new position and trusts the browser's
to handle the DOM reordering. This is efficient for most list sizes, but for very large lists (thousands of items) virtualisation is a better tool than key optimisation.

Keys work outside of explicit lists too. Any sibling JSX elements that should be treated as having stable identities can have keys:

When a component unmounts unexpectedly, a key change is often the cause. Signs:
A component that has no transition or animation disappears and reappears briefly
with an empty dependency array runs more than once between user actions
Form input values reset when unrelated state updates
Scroll position resets on a list item that was not moved
React DevTools shows the Fiber tree in real time. Enable 'Record why each component rendered' in the Profiler settings and look for components that show 'Key changed' as the render reason.

Keys give the reconciler stable identities for list items, enabling correct and efficient reconciliation regardless of position. A stable key (from a database ID or other business identifier) means React reuses the existing Fiber — preserving state and skipping unnecessary DOM work. A changed key means React destroys and recreates the Fiber — resetting state and running all mount effects. Understanding this distinction explains most key-related bugs in React applications.
Keys and Performance: What Keys Actually Optimise
A common misconception is that keys improve performance by themselves. Keys only prevent
bugs from positional mismatch. The actual performance question is whether React can skip calling the component function and diffing its output — that is the job of

That said, correct keys do avoid unnecessary UPDATE effects on unchanged items. Without keys, a prepend causes React to update every list item's DOM text content (because positional match finds diffs in all n positions). With keys, only the truly changed items get DOM work — the rest produce zero DOM writes even though their component functions may still run.

Keyed list items that animate in or out benefit from explicit keys because animation libraries hook into mount and unmount events. A key change triggers unmount, which runs exit animations. Stable keys mean the component stays mounted through re-renders, allowing continuous animations:
Test key behaviour the same way you test any reconciliation concern — by checking that component state survives a re-render with stable keys and resets with changed keys:
Testing key behaviour is straightforward with React Testing Library's
helper. Prefer these integration-level tests over trying to inspect Fiber internals directly.
Use this decision table when choosing keys:
Items from a database or API
at creation time (never at render time)
Static list that never reorders or gains middle insertions
Need to intentionally reset component state
Animated items that need exit animations

The rule of thumb: every item in a dynamic list needs a key that comes from the data, not from the render. If your data has a unique identifier (it almost always does), use it. If it does not, generate one at creation time with
